themes.name: Ghosts in the Machine
themes.tagline: When the past gets reconstructed, cloned, or challenged by technology — what's left of the real thing?

items.title: Greatest Speech in American History?
items.description:
This video presents an AI reconstruction of Abraham Lincoln’s Gettysburg Address, recreated with care to honor its historical legacy.
Using advanced voice and audio modeling, the speech is brought to life in a way modern audiences can experience firsthand.
The setting and tone aim to reflect the solemn atmosphere of the original 1863 ceremony.
This reconstruction does not alter the message, but seeks to preserve the emotional weight of the moment.
Viewers are invited to imagine the crowd, the ba

items.title: We Recreated The Oldest Surviving Globe
An AI Company Wants to Clone Me
items.description:
Check out Lindsay Ellis' new video only on Nebula: https://nebula.tv/videos/lindsayellis-bluey-adults?ref=jaredhenderson

AI companies are selling products that purport to let you speak with your dead loved ones or your favorite creators. And one wanted to make an AI clone of me.

Read my full letter: https://jaredhenderson.substack.com/p/a-company-wanted-to-make-an-ai-clone

SOURCES

Technopoly by Neil Postman

https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2949882125000398?via%3Dihub

items.title: Forgotten Goliath Mawtrain Pulled from the Ocean Floor Back to Life Full Rebuilt!
items.description:
Forgotten Goliath Mawtrain Pulled from the Ocean Floor Back to Life Full Rebuilt!

Goliath Mawtrain returns from the depths in one of the most incredible restoration projects ever imagined. After spending decades abandoned underwater, this massive machine is recovered from the ocean floor with extreme rust, severe corrosion, broken components, faded paint, and years of damage caused by saltwater exposure.

Watch the complete journey as the Goliath Mawtrain is carefully recovered, fully dismantle

themes.name: The Myth vs. The Moment
themes.tagline: History has a PR problem — these videos are here to set the record straight.

items.title: Why THIS Became The Most Hated Bottled Water
items.description:
In the late 1990s, Americans started turning away from sugary soda and embracing what seemed like a healthier alternative: bottled water. Sensing the massive shift in consumer habits, Coca-Cola jumped into the booming market in 1999 with Dasani.

But what followed wasn’t so much a success story as it was a branding disaster. From its controversial sourcing to its unusual taste, Dasani triggered a public backlash and quickly became one of the most criticized bottled water brands ever.

items.title: Attempting to Make "The Creature" from Frankenstein (2025)
items.description:
What's that??? ME? Making an actually relevant costume? *sharp gasp that causes me to choke on my spit a little*
That's right. It's not often that I am utterly entranced and motivated to drop what I'm doing and create a new cosplay, but watching Guillermo Del Toro's Frankenstein got my creature-loving brain FIRED UP.
So please enjoy this lengthy quest of me trying many new methods, wig styling, prosthetic makeup, and more!
I am taking December off, so I will see you in the new year!

The post Pianist Joey Alexander
morning_soundtrack.vibe_check: Like sitting cross-legged between two worlds — the chaos of a city that never sleeps and the stillness of a place where time moves like incense smoke.

global_silver_linings.title: Pharaoh’s Daughters Were Skilled at Archery 4,000 Years ago, Reveals New Research
global_silver_linings.snippet: For decades, experts have disputed whether weapons found in ancient burial chambers of Egyptian princesses were actually their tools or placed there in a purely symbolic way. Now, a reassessment of five royal women who lived 4,000 years ago showed they were skilled at archery. Strong muscle attachments and healed fractures were observed in multiple […] The post Pharaoh’s Daughters Were Skilled at Archery 4,000 Years ago, Reveals New Research appeared first on Good News Network .
global_silver_linings.reason: New research revealing that ancient Egyptian princesses were accomplished archers 4,000 years ago reminds us that human skill, strength, and ambition have never been the sole preserve of one gender. History keeps offering up these quiet corrections, and every one of them expands our sense of what people are capable of.

discovery_articles.title: World's Largest Tortilla in Tlalnepantla de Baz, Mexico
discovery_articles.snippet: On November 25, 2013, Ripley México presented the recognition to Tlanepantla municipality for presenting the largest tortilla in the world. It was 3 meters in diameter and weighed 120 kilos; but it was not made to be eaten that day, but to be mounted on a fiberglass structure and sculpted on it. The sculptures were made by art teacher Enrique Ramos from corn dough and represent everything that Mexico means.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: A 120-kilo tortilla was never meant to be eaten — it was a canvas, sculpted into an edible (well, technically) monument to Mexican identity by a single art teacher working in corn dough. There's something quietly radical about turning the most ordinary staple into the grandest possible gesture.

discovery_articles.title: Roadside America Museum in Hillsboro, Texas
discovery_articles.snippet: Carroll Estes has spent his life fixated on the past. Professionally, he made his living through his restoration shop Carroll’s Classic Auto Body. Personally, he was snapping up pieces of 1950s memorabilia, relics, and iconography before they were even retro, amassing an impressive enough collection by 1975 to begin showing it off to the public.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: Carroll Estes was collecting 1950s Americana before nostalgia had even decided the '50s were worth missing — which means he wasn't chasing a trend, he was just paying attention when everyone else was throwing things away. That's the rarest kind of curator.

discovery_articles.title: Major Adams Monument in Johannesburg, California
discovery_articles.snippet: The X-15 rocket plane program is barely remembered today, but it was the first venture by crewed craft to the edge of space. The rocket plane, carrying a human pilot, would be carried to high altitude under the wing of a modified B-52. The plane would then be released, and the pilot would initiate a flight plan, firing the rocket engine and commonly flying up to the edge of space.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The X-15 pilots were technically astronauts — touching the edge of space — yet the program has nearly dissolved from public memory, overshadowed by the moonshot it quietly helped make possible. A monument on a California roadside may be all that marks the spot where that story almost disappeared entirely.

discovery_articles.title: Kilmodan Stones in Glendaruel, Scotland
discovery_articles.snippet: Located in the quiet village of Glendaruel in the Argyll and Bute region of Scotland, the Kilmodan Stones are a collection of ancient Pictish stones dating back to the medieval period. These weathered monoliths, primarily composed of local sandstone, are adorned with intricate carvings and designs, including Celtic motifs and Pictish symbols. Believed to have been created between the 7th and 9th centuries CE, the Kilmodan Stones are thought to have served various purposes within early Pictish society.
discovery_articles.ferns_note: The Picts left no written language, so these carved sandstone slabs in a sleepy Scottish glen are effectively the closest thing to a sentence they ever finished. Whatever they were trying to say has been weathering slowly for over a thousand years, still legible enough to unsettle you.

featured_read.title: Borges on How to Conquer Time
featured_read.snippet: "Time is the substance I am made of. Time is a river which sweeps me along, but I am the river; it is a tiger which destroys me, but I am the tiger; it is a fire which consumes me, but I am the fire."
featured_read.blurb: Borges offers something stranger and more consoling than the usual advice about mortality: not how to manage time, but how to recognize that you *are* it. Set aside the half-hour and let him dissolve the distance between the river and the person drowning in it.

garden_note.note: High summer lingers deliciously late in Zürich tonight — the last warmth pressed into stone walls, lavender still humming with bees even as the light fades. Step outside before 21:53 and catch that long amber dusk dissolving over the Albiskette; this is the season's unhurried gift.
in_season: Courgettes — harvest daily before they become marrows
Deadhead roses to coax a second flush
garden_note.sky_tonight: A slim waxing crescent hangs low in the southwest after dusk — look just below it for Jupiter brightening as the sky deepens.
garden_note.sun_times: The sun sets at 21:17 tonight, with dusk easing in around 21:53.

puzzle.label: The Evening Enigma
puzzle.prompt: A man carefully reads every word on the menu, orders confidently, and yet when the food arrives he cannot eat a single thing on his plate. The restaurant has made no mistakes, and the man is not ill. Why not?
puzzle.answer: The man is a professional food critic who ordered on behalf of his dining companion — the meal was always intended for someone else.
puzzle.hint: Consider who the food was actually meant for.

previous_puzzle.label: Fern's Morning Riddle
previous_puzzle.prompt: While on my way to St. Ives, I saw a man with 7 wives. Each wife had 7 sacks. Each sack had 7 cats. Each cat had 7 kittens. Kittens, cats, sacks, and wives. How many were going to St. Ives?

fern_data.greeting: The sky outside is doing that particular August thing — half tangerine, half ash — and it felt like the right moment to sit with questions: whether the oldest surviving globe holds stranger secrets than we remember, whether Regency women were actually that unhinged (jury's still out), and what Borges, of all people, might tell us about evenings like this one.
fern_data.top_pick_title: Old Ghosts, Wet Hems & Borrowed Time
